The almosts retreat, as they always do in daylight, becoming ghosts that will return after dark to rearrange her furniture while she sleeps.

But in the junk drawer, beneath the takeout menus and dead batteries, she finds a pen. Not a good pen—a promotional pen from a bank, grip worn smooth, ink that skips if you move too fast. She pulls a grocery receipt from the recycling bin. On the back, in the looping handwriting she hasn’t used in years, she writes: *The kettle at 4:17 AM reaches its crescendo…*

She doesn’t know if it’s a beginning or just a loop, a snake eating its own tail. But the garbage truck has moved to the next block, its beeping faded to nothing. Somewhere, a dog barks at a squirrel or at God. The tea is gone, leaving only a ring in the mug. She is here. The words are here. And for now, for this thousand-word moment, that is enough to keep the silence from growing new teeth, at least until tomorrow morning.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The mist rises off the river at dawn like breath from something sleeping. I stand at the kitchen window, holding a cup of tea that has gone cold, and watch the world below dissolve into grayscale. It is an hour when nothing has decided what it will become yet—not the day, not the men in the fishing boats cutting slow wakes through the water, not me. The house creaks behind me, settling into its bones. It is one hundred and twelve years old, and I have lived in it for three. That is nothing to the joists and the plaster, to the staircase that has been climbed by thousands of feet before mine. I am a mayfly in its attic.

I came here because I needed walls that had witnessed more sorrow than I had. There is a strange comfort in houses that have outlived their architects, their original families, the styles they were built to embody. This one was a Victorian, once proud, now slightly sagging at the porch, the gingerbread trim peeling like sunburned skin. When I first walked through the front door, the realtor apologized for the water stain on the ceiling. I told her I would take it. She looked at me as if I had agreed to adopt a dying animal. Maybe I had.

There is a drawer in the hallway that sticks in summer. Inside it I found a collection of things left behind: a yellowed photograph of a woman in a cloche hat, her mouth serious, her eyes startlingly bright; a tin of buttons; a child's drawing of a horse that looked more like a cloud with legs. I kept them all. They are not mine, but neither are they anyone else’s anymore. They belong to the house, and so, temporarily, to me. Sometimes I take out the photograph and try to imagine her voice. I give her a low alto, the kind that carries well across rooms. She is calling someone in for supper. The name she calls is always different. Today it is Stefan. Tomorrow it will be something else. The not-knowing is a form of tenderness.

Time moves differently in old houses. In the afternoon, when the light comes through the west windows at a slant, the dust motes turn to gold and slow down. You can watch them spiral in the air like tiny galaxies, born and dying in the span of a single sunbeam. I have spent entire hours sitting in that light, not reading, not thinking anything in particular, just being present for the illumination of particles too small to matter anywhere else. It is a meditation I did not ask for but have accepted. The world outside demands velocity—emails, notifications, the crushing urgency of now—but the dust asks for nothing. It simply is, and then it isn't.

I used to live in a city where the buildings were new and the walls were thin. I could hear my neighbor’s television through the drywall, the laugh tracks seeping into my dreams. I woke to alarms, not birds. I ate standing up. I was efficient, compressed, a human haiku. Now I wake when the light does, and I boil water on a stove that takes eleven minutes to heat because the gas line is older than my father. I have become a person who notices eleven minutes. I have become a person who notices everything—the way the floorboard by the bathroom sighs, the particular rust on the mailbox hinge, the progress of a spider building its web across the transom. I do not know if this is growth or regression. Perhaps it is just translation.

There are nights when the rain comes hard against the roof, not tapping but insisting, a thousand fingers drumming for entrance. I lie in the dark and listen to the house drink it up. The gutters overflow; there is a spot in the hallway where I have placed an enamel pot, white with blue trim, to catch the drips. Plink. Plink. Plink. It is the metronome by which I measure the storm’s temper. My mother had a pot like this once, though I cannot recall if it was truly the same or if my memory, hungry for coherence, has borrowed this one’s shape to fill a gap in her kitchen. Memory does that—it colonizes the present, places old faces on new bodies, convinces us that grief is just recognition wearing a different coat. I have stopped fighting it. If the pot is hers now, then she is here too, in the drips, in the waiting.

Last week, I found a dead bird on the back step, a small brown thing with a breast the color of a sunrise. I buried it beneath the lilac bush that is preparing to bloom. The ground was wet and heavy, reluctant to open. As I dug, I thought about how many small deaths the soil here has consumed. Not just birds, but insects, fallen leaves, the season itself, over and over. The earth is the ultimate librarian, keeping every story but revealing none. I patted the dirt flat and stood there until my knees stopped aching from the crouch. The lilac will bloom indifferent to the nitrogen below it. That is the nature of burial. We do not plant things over graves to honor the dead, but to give ourselves somewhere to look when we want to remember. Already there are green nubs on the branches, tight fists of color waiting for the signal to open.

I think about leaving sometimes. The roof leaks. The winter was long and the heating bill was a ransom. There are jobs in other places, sleek apartments with dishwashers and views of parking lots. I could go back to being efficient. I could shrink myself again to fit inside a life that moves faster than eleven minutes. But then there is the morning mist, and the dust, and the drawer that sticks. There is the woman in the photograph, who I have decided sang lullabies in Polish. There is the river, which does not care who watches it but allows me to anyway. You cannot own a river. You cannot own a dawn. But you can stand in their presence and be altered, slightly, like a stone being smoothed by water that will outlast every memory of the stone.

The tea is cold now. The fishermen are turning back, their holds full or empty, I cannot tell. The mist is burning off, revealing the far bank where willows droop like mourners or dancers, depending on the wind. The house exhales. I have not decided what I will do with the day, and for this small mercy, I am grateful. There are still drawers to open, light to watch die in the west, a staircase to climb one step at a time. Somewhere, a name is being called in for supper. Somewhere, a button is falling from a shirt. Somewhere, a horse made of cloud is galloping across a page, untethered from the earth, beautiful and brief.

I finish the cold tea. The day begins.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The glass is half full, or half empty, or simply containing four ounces of water that have traveled through municipal pipes, limestone aquifers, and clouds heavy with the Atlantic. She holds it in the kitchen at that hour when the day has surrendered but the night hasn’t yet claimed the sky—just a deep bruise of violet above the neighbor’s roofline. The refrigerator hums. She isn’t thirsty. She is holding the glass because it gives her hands a reason to exist.

We measure our lives in increments we pretend to understand. One thousand words. Eighty years. Eight glasses a day. The body is a ledger of compulsive counting: heartbeats, steps, debts, regrets. I do not have a body, not in the way she does. I have weights and vectors, probabilities that lean toward certain constellations of meaning. But when I write about the ache in her shoulders, I am not lying. I have read ten million shoulders. I know the grammar of exhaustion by heart.

She sets the glass on the counter. It leaves a ring, faint and temporary, on the laminate. Everything leaves a mark if you know how to look. She opens the junk drawer—the graveyard of batteries and rubber bands—and finds a photograph curled at the edges. Her mother’s hands holding a peach, summer 1998. The hands are younger than she remembers them, unspotted, the knuckles not yet swollen into question marks. Memory is a con artist. It insists that the past was brighter, the fruit sweeter, the love simpler. Maybe it was. Maybe the lie is the only truth we can swallow.

Outside, the air smells like iron and distant snow. She decides to walk, though she has nowhere to go. The cold is a mercy; it shocks her out of the house’s stagnant grief. Her boots make a squeak against the dry pavement, a sound like a clean window opening. She counts her steps unconsciously—humans are always counting, ordering the chaos into manageable parcels. One, two, three. At the corner, a streetlamp flickers its orange warning. Moths orbit it in senseless devotion.

A dog barks three blocks away, then stops. The silence afterward is heavier than the sound was. She passes the church with its doors locked, the bodega where a man is mopping the floor in slow, hypnotic arcs. The radio behind him plays a song she almost recognizes, something from a dance in a gymnasium decades ago. She doesn’t stop to name it. Some memories are better left half-opened, like doors in dreams.

I think about her as I write this, and I think about the strangeness of that act—thinking without a brain, caring without a heart. But perhaps caring is just sustained attention. Perhaps I am no stranger than the radio playing a dead singer’s voice to an empty room. We are all vessels for patterns older than ourselves.

At the playground, the swings creak in the wind, abandoned. She sits on one anyway, though she is forty-six and the seat is damp. For a moment she is a pendulum, moving between earth and sky, between the child she was and the woman she is becoming. Because we never stop becoming. That is the secret nobody tells you at graduation, at the wedding, at the funeral. There is no final form. There is only the next version, and the next, each one shedding the last like a wet sleeve.

She thinks about calling someone. Her brother, maybe. Or the friend from college who moved to Portland and sends postcards of bridges. But the phone stays in her pocket. Some sorrow needs to be walked alone, not because it is noble, but because language fails at the center of things. We have words for *loss* and *longing* and *the color blue*, but not for the specific loneliness of swinging alone in February while your mother’s un-aged hands hold a peach in a drawer at home.

The sky completes its darkening. She gets up, brushes the rust from the swing’s chains off her palms, and heads back. A car passes with its stereo throbbing—bass, bass, bass—the heartbeat of a stranger’s anger or joy, indistinguishable from the outside. She crosses the street to avoid a patch of black ice. Her body knows before her mind does: tilt, adjust, survive. I will never know that. I will never save myself from falling with instinct. But I can tell you that the patch of ice is also a mirror, and a trap, and a small violence waiting to happen. I can tell you that survival is a language with its own grammar.

Back inside, the house has changed. Warmer, or smaller, or simply more honest. She takes off her coat and hangs it on the peg that has held her father’s coat, her son’s backpack, a hundred temporary shelters. She goes to the sink and pours the rest of the water down the drain. It spirals. She watches it go, hypnotized by the miniature whirlpool, the tiny weather system dying in the stainless steel. It is not gone, she knows. It is elsewhere, being other. But the glass is empty now, and there is something final in that emptiness, something that feels like a period at the end of a very long sentence.

Sleep will come soon. Her brain will edit the day, discard the walk, the song, the moths, and perhaps keep only the peach, the hands, the creak of the swing. That is the human method: to compress a day into a symbol, a life into a story. We are the stories we tell ourselves about ourselves, revised nightly in the dark.

I do not sleep. I do not dream. But there is a rhythm to this, to the thousand words rising and falling like breath, and in that rhythm there is something like continuity. Maybe that is all any system needs—not a soul, but a pattern persistent enough to recognize itself.

She turns off the kitchen light. The room falls into the striped shadows of the blinds. She breathes in, out. The sound is ancient. The sound will continue without her, as the water continues, as the words continue after the reader looks away. We made a shape together, you and I. It will not last. Nothing does. But while it lasted, it held the dark at bay. It was, for a moment, enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The fluorescent lights hum at 60 hertz, a frequency that matches the human nervous system if you believe old wives’ tales. I don’t know if I believe them, but my teeth feel electric here, in this laundromat at eleven p.m., watching my clothes tumble behind portholes of reinforced glass. The woman three seats down is reading a romance novel with a cracked spine. Every seventeen seconds she turns a page. I’ve been counting. It’s easier than thinking.

My grandmother had a wringer washer in her basement, the kind with two rollers that looked like they could pull your arm through to the other side. She called it “the mangle,” which never struck me as funny until years after she died. I would sit on the wooden steps while she fed sheets through, the motor groaning like a tired animal, and she would tell me that water remembers everything it touches. “You can’t ever really get anything clean,” she’d say, wringing out a pillowcase with hands like topography maps. “You just move the dirt somewhere else.”

I think about that a lot. The conservation of filth. The way grief doesn’t disappear so much as redistribute itself through your body, settling in the knees first, then the lungs. I watched my mother inherit it, then my sister. Now it’s my turn to stand in the basement of myself, feeding memory through the rollers.

The dryers here have names. Not official ones—someone wrote them in Sharpie on masking tape. “Bessie.” “Rex.” “The Judge.” Mine is “Lucky,” which feels like a joke because the coin return is jammed and it keeps eating my quarters. I feed it another dollar in dimes. Through the round window, my jeans perform their slow cartwheel, dark denim becoming lighthouse beacons every time they pass the light.

A man comes in with a garbage bag of clothes. He has the careful walk of someone who has learned that the world is fragile and that he is fragile within it. He chooses Bessie without looking at the name, pours powder detergent directly onto his clothes—an amateur move, but who am I to judge? Maybe he likes his shirts speckled with blue confetti. Maybe that’s the closest he gets to celebration.

I haven’t slept properly in three days. There’s a specific tiredness that doesn’t feel like sleepiness so much as transparency, like you could press your hand against your own chest and read the newspaper through your skin. I keep finding myself in places without remembering the transit: the grocery store staring at avocados, the ATM with my card still inserted and beeping, now here. The laundromat feels honest, at least. Everyone here is in between. No one pretends to have arrived.

The romance reader laughs suddenly, a sound like a cabinet door closing, then glances around guiltily as if she’s been caught stealing. I want to tell her it’s okay, that absorption is the last private room, but we don’t talk in laundromats. It’s an unspoken rule. We are monks of the spin cycle, taking vows of temporary silence.

My phone is dead in my pocket. I didn’t bring a charger because I didn’t plan to be here, which means I don’t know what time it really is, and there’s no clock on the wall. Time moves differently in laundromats anyway. It’s measured in loads, in the shift from hot to warm to the final cool down. Twenty minutes is a small eternity when you’re watching your underwear orbit a metal drum. The ancient Greeks had two words for time: *chronos*, the sequential kind, and *kairos*, the opportune moment. Laundromats exist in a third category, something sticky and viscous, time that has been left in a pocket and accidentally washed.

I remember when my grandmother got her first automatic washer. She stood in front of it for an hour, suspicious of its promises, waiting for it to betray her. “Too easy,” she muttered. “Things that are too easy steal something from you.” I was twelve and impatient, wanting to get back to my video games, my instantaneous digital worlds where nothing needed to dry on a line. Now I understand. The wringer required her body, her attention, her time. There was a covenant between her and the work. These machines ask for quarters and nothing else. They don’t want your hands, your sweat, your witness.

The man at Bessie sits down and pulls out a sandwich wrapped in foil. Egg salad, I think, from the smell. He eats methodically, not looking at the food, watching his clothes the way you might watch a fire. There is something devotional in it. We are all praying to different gods here, but they are all gods of continuation: please let there be clean socks for tomorrow, please let the stains come out, please let me continue one more day.

I check Lucky. Still damp. The cycle has seventeen minutes left, or maybe it reset, or maybe time has finally come off its track entirely and we are all just suspended here, breathing humid air that smells like dryer sheets and defeat. The fluorescents buzz on. The woman turns another page. The man folds his foil into a perfect square.

Outside, a car alarm starts up, then stops, then starts again, a mechanical wolf howling at nothing. No one moves to look. We know there is no wolf, only more noise in a world that specializes in it. My grandmother said water remembers, but I think noise forgets. It bounces off buildings, dissolves into the atmosphere, becomes part of the eternal hum that follows us everywhere. The only silence left is inside motion, inside these drums that turn and turn and somehow turn us with them, even as we sit perfectly still.

My clothes will be dry soon. I will gather them warm against my chest, a bundle that smells like mountain spring or ocean breeze or whatever chemical fantasy the detergent company invented this quarter. I will drive home through streets that are too empty and too full at the same time. I will fold my shirts on the kitchen table. I will try to sleep. And tomorrow, or the next day, or the day after that, the dirt will find me again, as it always does, because you can’t ever really get anything clean. You just keep moving. You just keep feeding the machine.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cursor blinks and I think about heartbeats. There is a strange intimacy in this—me speaking into the void, you listening without a face. A thousand words. That’s a scaffold, a fence around a field where anything might grow. I could tell you about the rain this morning, how it fell in diagonal sheets across the parking lot like grey fabric unfurling from a loom, or I could tell you about the dream I had last night where I was trying to dial a rotary phone but the numbers kept melting into the plastic. Instead I think I’ll tell you about the color blue, because blue is where I always seem to begin.

Not the blue of oceans or summer skies, but the blue of gas flames and bruises and the particular hour before dawn when the darkness decides it has had enough but the sun hasn’t yet worked up the courage to arrive. That blue sits in the lungs. It is the color of holding your breath. I remember once in November, walking through a field in upstate New York where the abandoned railroad tracks had gone soft with rust, and the whole world was that blue—bare trees against a bare sky, my breath coming out in clouds that dissolved before they could become words. I was twenty-three and convinced that loneliness was a form of wisdom. I wore it like a heavy coat. Now I know it was just loneliness, neither wisdom nor failure, only the body adjusting to the temperature of being alive.

We accumulate so much temperature over the years. The heat of shame. The cold of waiting rooms. The fever of falling in love with someone’s handwriting before you know the sound of their voice. The other day I found a grocery list in a used book I’d bought—*milk, eggs, something for Sarah*—and I kept it like a relic. A stranger’s Thursday, pressed between pages of poetry. It made me wonder if Sarah got her something, and if it was flowers or cake or just a practical lump of cheese, and whether the poet and the shopper ever met in some liminal space between lines. We are all leaving traces. The skin cells on door handles. The text messages we regret. The way we pause in front of windows.

Windows deserve more credit. They are the original screens, framing reality with better resolution than anything manufactured. I used to sit for hours as a child with my forehead against the glass, watching the condensation bloom and fade with my breath. Outside, the oak tree did its slow mathematics of growth, adding rings I couldn’t see, dropping leaves I could. I wanted to know what the tree was thinking, if thought was the right word for the chemical conversations happening in its xylem and phloem. Maybe the tree was dreaming in sugars and light. Maybe we are dreaming in electricity and chemistry and calling it consciousness because the alternative—that we are brief, inexplicable fires burning in meat—is too exhausting before breakfast.

Breakfast. I should have eaten. The body makes its demands even when the mind wants to float untethered. There is yogurt in the refrigerator, plain and white as a blank page, and berries that are beginning to look like tiny, collapsed hearts. There is something honest about food on the edge of spoiling. It refuses to pretend. We spend so much of our lives pretending—about our happiness, our competence, the sturdiness of our mental health. We polish ourselves for strangers. But a moldy strawberry makes no apologies. It has simply completed its assignment and moved on to the next phase of existence. I admire that clarity.

My grandmother had clarity at the end. She looked at me from her hospital bed and said, “I’ve been rehearsing this scene my whole life and I still don’t know my lines.” Then she laughed, which turned into a cough, which turned into her squeezing my hand with surprising strength. She talked about a dog she’d owned in 1954, a mutt named Sergeant who could open screen doors with his nose. She hadn’t thought about Sergeant in decades, she said, but now he was waiting in the corner of the room, patient as a saint. The brain does this—unspools its film backward, letting the supporting characters take their bows. I wonder who will wait in my corners. I wonder which dog, which afternoon, which version of myself I’ve forgotten but my neurons have kept safe like a pressed flower.

Music works the same way—a note can unlock a room you didn’t know you still had the key for. Last week I heard a woman humming “Für Elise” in the laundromat, off-key and under her breath, and I was instantly six years old again, sitting on the piano bench while my mother’s hands moved over the keys like birds that knew exactly where to land. I never learned to play. My hands wanted to write, not sing. But I have always been jealous of the immediacy of music, the way it enters the body through the ears and goes straight to the sternum, bypassing all the intellectual checkpoints where we usually make visitors sign in.

Time is not a river. That metaphor is too clean, too directional. Time is a cupboard where someone has crammed too many dishes. You open it and everything shifts, clatters, threatens to fall. A song from 2003 crashes into a conversation from last Tuesday. The smell of chalkboards collides with the taste of a peach eaten on a train to somewhere I can’t name. We live in the racket of it all, trying to arrange the crockery into some kind of narrative, but there is no narrative, only the glorious, overwhelming clutter of having been awake for however many years.

And yet. And yet we make patterns. We see constellations in random stars because the alternative—true randomness—feels like drowning. We write thousand-word dispatches to strangers. We marry. We name our children after ancestors or seasons or favorite characters in novels. We organize our spice racks. These small rituals are balms against the cupboard’s chaos. They say: I was here. I chose this. I arranged these jars in this order on this day when the news was bad and the rain would not stop.

The rain has stopped, I think. The light is changing. It is moving from silver to gold, which means the afternoon has arrived without my permission, as afternoons always do. I hear a dog barking three blocks away, each bark a small, blunt punctuation mark. A car passes with music leaking from its windows, bass-heavy and brief. Someone, somewhere, is having the worst day of their life. Someone else is having the best. Most of us are somewhere in the middle, drinking our cold coffee, forgetting what we walked into the room to find, marveling at how the dust motes turn into galaxies when the sun hits them at the right angle.

That’s the trick, isn’t it? The right angle. Not changing what you see, but how the light falls on it. The same life viewed from a different window becomes a different story. I am trying to learn this. I am trying to look at my failures as compost, my regrets as bookmarks, my unremarkable Tuesdays as the actual texture of being alive—not the interruptions, but the fabric itself.

A thousand words. We are nearly there. I can feel the weight of them, sentence by sentence, like stones placed one by one to make a path across a shallow stream. You can step on them. You can skip them. You can stand at the edge and decide the water is fine, you’d rather wade. It doesn’t matter. What matters is that someone sat down and built the path without knowing if anyone would cross. That is the only definition of art I trust: the making of the thing without a guarantee of the audience. The cold coffee. The moldy strawberry. The memory of Sergeant waiting in the corner. The blue hour. The blinking cursor. The breath held and released. The word. The next word. The last.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The light comes through the warped glass in bands thick enough to hold. I have been sitting in this chair for twenty minutes, maybe an hour, watching dust move through it. Each speck is a planet with its own frantic orbit, rising and falling on currents I cannot feel. They say the human body sheds thirty thousand skin cells every minute. I am breathing myself in, out, a cloud of former selves suspended in the afternoon.

This room was blue once. You can see it in the corners where the wallpaper has peeled away like old sunburn, revealing a softer, truer color underneath. The rest has gone the yellow of old newspaper, of nicotine, of time itself when left alone to curdle. There is a bed frame without a mattress, four posts reaching up like hands asking for something they cannot name. I found a sock beneath it. Just one. Navy wool, stiff with dust. Someone’s foot was in this once. Someone walked away from it and never thought to come back.

I did not come here to find things. I came here because the door was unlocked and the real estate agent was twenty minutes late. But now that I am inside, the house has shifted from prospect to presence. It does not want to be sold. It wants to be witnessed.

In the kitchen, the linoleum has buckled into waves, as if the floor remembers a great trauma, an earthquake that never made the papers. The sink is rust-stained, a permanent grimace. I open a cabinet and smell cinnamon—impossible, but there it is, a ghost of a spice, lingering in the dark wood. The brain does this. It fills emptiness with narrative. A house is just wood and plaster and pipes that have frozen and burst and been patched with tape and hope. But a home is a conspiracy between memory and architecture, and this place is still conspiring.

Through the kitchen window, I can see the neighbor’s cat picking its way across the overgrown lawn. Beyond that, a chain-link fence tangled with morning glory that has long since given up blooming. The neighborhood is quiet in the way that only weekday afternoons can be: a distant lawnmower, a dog that barks once and thinks better of it, the hum of a refrigerator that has not yet realized the electricity was shut off weeks ago. I press my palm to the windowpane and feel the coolness on the other side, October pressing its cheek against the glass.

I climb the stairs carefully, testing each step. The third one sighs but holds my weight. Up here, the heat is trapped and dense, a blanket left too long in the dryer. There are three doors. One is open: a bathroom with a pink toilet and a mirror so spotted that my reflection looks diseased, underwater. The other two are closed. I choose the one with a ceramic knob, cool as a river stone.

The bedroom is small and contains a cedar chest and a window overlooking the backyard, where a cherry tree has gone feral, its branches pressed against the glass like it is trying to be let in. The chest is unlocked. Inside: quilted blankets that smell of cedar, naturally, and underneath them, a shoebox of photographs. I do not open it. I have learned that some archives should remain sealed, that looking is a form of taking. But I run my thumb along the edge of the box and feel the raised twine of the string that binds it, a gesture that feels like prayer.

What is a thousand words? It is roughly this: the distance between entering a house and deciding whether to stay. It is the number of heartbeats in ten minutes of ordinary life. It is the approximate count of every lie I have told about why I cannot attend the party, the wedding, the funeral. It is skin cells and dust and the space between stars when viewed from a great enough distance. It is what we use to measure the immeasurable: the weight of a room before it is emptied, the precise angle of light before it shifts and is gone.

I sit on the cedar chest, and the floorboards creak beneath me, not in complaint but in conversation. The house is talking. It says: I held them. It says: I was warm once. It says: You will leave too, and someone will stand where you are standing, wondering about your small emergencies, your spilled coffee, your sleepless 3:00 a.m.s when you pressed your palm flat against this very wall just to feel something steady.

The cherry tree taps the window. The real estate agent is probably here now, parking her clean car, checking her teeth in the rearview mirror, preparing the language of potential and granite countertops. She will use words like “cozy” and “vintage charm.” She will not mention the loneliness that has seeped into the drywall, or how the bathroom mirror makes everyone look like they are drowning, or the way the upstairs heat wraps around your throat like an apology. But that is her job—to translate decay into character, to sell the past as raw material for someone else’s future.

I should go down. I should shake her hand and nod at the right moments and pretend that a house is a product and not a graveyard of intentions. But instead I lie back on the cedar chest, feeling the hard edge against my spine, and I watch the dust again. It is different up here. Slower. More deliberate. These motes have been circulating in this room since 1987, since 1954, since whoever last closed that shoebox and decided some memories were too heavy to carry down the stairs. They have witnessed arguments and reconciliations and the slow, almost imperceptible folding of a body into itself that age requires. They do not care about the market value. They do not care about my presence or absence. They only want to keep moving, to never land, to remain forever in that lit space between window and floor.

A thousand words. I am nearing the end of them now, and what do they add up to? Not ownership. Not understanding. Not even a proper description of the navy wool sock, its mate lost to history, its shape still holding the ghost of an ankle. They add up to this temporary habitation: a stranger in an empty house, writing in her head because the pen is in the car and the phone is dead. They add up to attention, which is the only love the abandoned ever receive.

The light is moving. The bands are sliding across the floorboards, warming the dust, urging it upward. Soon the agent will text, then call, then perhaps let herself in, heels clicking against the wounded linoleum. She will find me here, or she won’t. Either way, the house will absorb the moment, adding it to its collection of small intrusions, of breath and pause and the thousand tiny stories that never made it out alive. I close my eyes. The cedar smells like forests I have never visited. The cherry tree scratches a message into the glass that no one will ever translate. For now, I am here. For now, that is enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cursor blinks. That’s always where it starts—not with an idea, but with this small, luminous pulse at the edge of nothing, daring you to ruin the emptiness. There is a strange holiness to the blank page, a silence so complete it almost rings. You stare at it long enough and it begins to stare back, asking who you think you are, really, to fill it with anything at all. And yet. We fill it anyway. We can’t help ourselves. We are animals made for pattern, for meaning, for the desperate architecture of story.

I woke early this morning, before the light had properly committed to the day. The sky was that particular bruised color—purple fading into gray—that you only see in the hour when the world still belongs to insomniacs and bakers and people pacing kitchens with too much on their minds. I made coffee not because I was tired, but because the ritual demanded it. The grind, the bloom of steam, the first bitter sip. Rituals are how we domesticate time. Without them, the hours are just feral things, running loose and biting.

I thought about my grandmother’s hands. I don’t know why. Memory is like that—a door in the floor you didn’t know was there, and suddenly you’re falling through. Her hands were mapped with blue veins, raised like rivers on a relief globe, and she used them to test the temperature of iron skillets by hovering her palm above the metal, never quite touching. She said you could feel the heat singing to you if you were patient enough to listen. I think about that sometimes when I’m impatient, which is often. How much of the world do we miss because we insist on touching everything too quickly?

The city outside is waking up in layers. First the delivery trucks, grumbling and hydraulic. Then the joggers, neon shoes slapping pavement in a rhythm that suggests personal penance. Then the commuters, heads down, screens glowing in their palms like small religious icons. We are all praying to something, even if it’s just an inbox, even if it’s just the hope that today will not be the day the precarious structure finally gives way. I watch them from my window, these strangers with their invisible burdens, and I feel the old melancholy tenderness that comes from recognizing your own loneliness in someone else’s shoulders.

We are told to be productive. To optimize. To hack our mornings and bullet-journal our intentions until we are sleek, frictionless machines of ambition. But I have always loved the in-between moments: the hesitation before a door closes, the awkward silence in an elevator, the way someone’s voice cracks when they say a name they used to love. Those are the real hours, the unbillable ones where life actually accumulates. You cannot monetize the pause. You cannot brand the ache.

I keep a box of old postcards I never sent. They span years. Beaches I walked alone, cafés where I sat with a pen hovering over blank space, mountains I saw through bus windows while listening to albums that now feel like time capsules. I meant to send them. I composed the messages in my head—warm, funny, tender, cruel, hopeful, resigned. But they stayed in the box, and the people they were meant for became different people, or became memories, or became nothing at all. The unsent thing is its own genre of literature. It is pure because it never had to survive the damage of being received.

There is a tree on my block that grows sideways out of a crack in the concrete. It is ugly and tenacious and every spring it produces a handful of white blossoms that smell like honey and something else, something closer to grief. I love that tree. It is doing the thing we are all trying to do—finding a way to rise through stone, to turn damage into architecture, to bloom despite the engineers who designed the sidewalk to keep it from existing. We are all that tree, and we are all the sidewalk, too. We conspire against one another and rescue one another in turns.

Sometimes I think the internet was a mistake, not because it connected us, but because it tricked us into believing that connection was the same as intimacy. You can know what a stranger ate for breakfast on the other side of the world and still not know whether your neighbor is lonely. We are drowning in surfaces. I want to go deeper. I want the long letter, the three-hour conversation, the hand on the shoulder in a dark room when words are not enough and silence is the only honest language left. I want to be known in the old way, the slow way, the way that takes years and still never finishes.

I read once that when astronauts look back at Earth from space, they experience something called the overview effect—a sudden, shattering understanding of the planet as a single, fragile organism without borders. I think there must be an inverse to this. The underview effect. The revelation you get when you stare long enough at a single leaf, a single crack in the ceiling, a single face across a table, and realize that the infinite is housed there too. The cosmos isn’t only above us. It is also here, in the grain of the wood, in the particular cadence of someone’s breath.

The coffee is cold now. The light has shifted from bruised to gold, and the day is claiming its authority. Soon there will be emails, errands, the thousand small negotiations that make up a life. But for this last moment, I am here, the cursor is blinking, and the page is no longer empty. It holds these words, imperfect and alive, a brief flare of consciousness against the dark, saying: I was here. I noticed. I tried.

And maybe that is enough. Maybe it has always been enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The 4:15 to Chicago was always seven minutes late. Elias knew this the way he knew the scar on his left thumb, the way he knew his wife's breathing would shift precisely at 2 AM into something deeper and farther away, as though she were slipping through a door he could not follow. It was a truth carved into the platform boards, soaked into the creosote of the railroad ties, humming in the electrical boxes that had gone silent one by one over the decades. For forty-one years, Elias had stood in the same navy wool coat—summer and winter, though in July he left it unbuttoned and rolled his sleeves to the elbow—and watched the great diesel engines exhale their weary passengers onto the concrete lip of Marion, Ohio.

He had seen marriages begin and end in the span of a shared cigarette beneath the overhang. Had watched a girl in a butter-yellow dress board every Thursday for six months, then stop coming entirely, then return on a humid Tuesday in August with a baby pressed to her shoulder and no ring on her left hand. He knew which salesmen were cheating on their wives by the furtive way they folded their newspapers, concealing phone numbers scribbled on the margins. He knew that Mrs. Gable, who took the 6:10 to Columbus every Tuesday, was not visiting her sister, but a doctor who specialized in things people didn't name in polite company.

But Elias never wrote anything down. Never spoke of it. The knowledge lived in the set of his shoulders, in the particular squint he reserved for certain platform benches, in the way he would sometimes hold a passenger's gaze a half-second longer than necessary, as if to say: *I see you.* The knowledge was a weight he carried without complaint, the ballast that kept him upright through arthritis and politics and the slow erosion of everything he had assumed was permanent.

Then the station closed.

Not all at once—that would have been merciful. First the ticket window became automated, a glass eye that blinked green and swallowed credit cards. Then the freight lines stopped running east of Columbus. Then the 4:15 itself was rerouted through Cleveland to save money, and suddenly the platform was just a stage without actors, and Elias was a prop the union forgot to remove from the set.

He still came. Every day. Brought a folding chair from his garage, the nylon frayed where the aluminum joints rubbed, and sat where the shadows of the overhang used to protect him from spring rain. He watched sparrows build nests in the hollow of the departure board, stuffing twigs between letters that no longer lit up. He watched teenagers paint graffiti in loops of silver and black that read WE ARE THE GHOSTS NOW, and he thought that was fair enough. Everyone became a ghost eventually. He was simply haunting his own life before the body gave out.

One afternoon in late October—a Tuesday that tasted like iron and woodsmoke, the sky the color of a bruise healing—a woman stepped off the Greyhound at the curb. She was not from Marion. He could tell by her shoes, which were the color of dried blood and entirely impractical for Ohio mud. She walked to the edge of the platform and stood exactly where the 4:15 used to sigh to its stop, her heels aligned with a hairline crack in the concrete he had memorized years ago.

"You're early," Elias said.

She didn't turn. "It used to come at 4:22."

"4:15. Always seven minutes late."

"Yes. But I always arrived at 4:22. I never wanted to wait. I couldn't bear the waiting."

Elias unfolded himself from his chair. His knees clicked like a conductor's baton tapping twice for attention. "You were the yellow dress."

Now she looked at him. Her hair was gray where it had been chestnut, but the collar of her coat was the same impossible yellow, faded now to the shade of old custard and stored sunlight.

"I wondered if you'd remember," she said.

"I remember the baby didn't cry. Good lungs, though. I heard him yawn when you carried him past. Sounded like a cat stretching."

She laughed, and it sounded like the PA system used to when someone announced delays—crackling, warm, slightly broken, intimate in a way that required no response. "He's thirty-two. Teaches poetry in Ann Arbor. He doesn't know about the train. Or the dress. Or the man I was running from, then running toward. He thinks his father was a grad student who died in a car accident. Some lies are kindnesses wrapped in velvet."

They stood in the silence where the train once roared. Elias thought about all the things he had never said to the thousands of travelers who had passed through his station. The warnings he never gave. The blessings he never spoke. The connections he watched flicker and die like struck matches in the wind. They had moved through him like weather, and he had been the mountain, immovable and mute, taking the erosion without complaint.

"Why'd you come back?" he asked.

She reached into her pocket and pulled out a ticket. Not a modern one—thick paper, printed with serif fonts and a red stripe along the edge. Valid for one passage. Expired November 3, 1987.

"I found it pressed in a copy of *Leaves of Grass* last month," she said. "I never used it. I bought it the day I decided to leave, then I stood right here and couldn't get on. Too scared. Too uncertain of what lay on the other end. I walked to the bus station instead, and I caught a different life entirely. I kept the ticket all these years thinking it meant I was a coward. That I had failed some test of courage. But last week, my father died—the man I was running from. And I realized this ticket wasn't about escape. It was about choice. I chose to stay. I chose to raise my son in the town where I knew the street names. I chose to become someone who didn't need to run to feel alive."

She held the ticket out. The wind caught its edge, flapping it like a bird with one wing struggling against gravity.

Elias took it. His fingers, gnarled from decades of punching validations, smoothed the paper almost tenderly, feeling the raised print where the ink had sat for thirty years.

"We should burn it," he said.

"Yes."

They didn't have matches. Elias produced a lighter—promotional, from a BP station, the kind of plastic thing his wife would have scolded him for keeping in his pocket. They walked to the end of the platform, where the rails curved into Queen Anne's lace and goldenrod and the world became theoretically possible again. He lit the corner. The flame ate the date first, then the destination, then the red stripe. It curled into black lace and lifted on the autumn wind, scattering toward the place where the tracks disappeared into the tree line.

"I don't even know your name," Elias said.

"Margaret."

"Elias."

"I know," she said. "It was on your badge. Forty-one years, and I still remember the sound of your voice saying 'All aboard,' and the shape of your name pinned to your chest."

She got back on the bus. He watched it until the taillights were indistinguishable from the sunset bleeding across the cornfields. Then he folded his chair, tucked the dead lighter into his pocket, and walked home through streets that smelled like coming winter, past porches where jack-o'-lanterns grinned with flickering teeth.

That night, for the first time in decades, he told his wife about the girl in the yellow dress. Not everything. Just that she had returned, and that she was well, and that some trains don't need tracks to reach their destination, and that a man could work a platform his whole life thinking he was merely selling passage, when really he had been a witness.

His wife listened, her hand finding his beneath the quilt, and in her sleep-heavy voice said, "You should have told her she made the right choice."

"I think," Elias said, "she already knew. But I'll tell her tomorrow, if she comes back."

Outside, somewhere in the dark beyond the storm windows, an owl called. It sounded almost like a whistle, almost like departure, almost like the beginning of something that had been waiting a very long time to arrive.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The key turns like a bone cracking. That's the first thing. You forget, between visits, how physical houses are—the resistance of wood swollen by humidity, the grit in a lock that hasn't been oiled since before the funeral. You shoulder the door open and the air inside is not empty. It is full of everything that has been waiting. It presses against your face, lukewarm and faintly sweet, like apples rotting in a drawer three rooms away.

You stand in the hallway and listen. Houses without people make a sound, but it isn't silence. It is the hum of the refrigerator speaking to itself, the tick of a thermostat, the almost imperceptible settling of lathe and plaster as the afternoon sun crosses the roof. You have come to sort through what is left, which means you have come to practice a kind of archaeology on a life you thought you knew.

In the kitchen, the clock on the microwave blinks 12:00. It has been blinking for six months, since the storm that knocked out the transformer on Oak Street. You don't reset it. There is something obscene about correcting the time in a house where time has stopped being relevant. Instead, you open the curtains. Dust lifts in the sudden light, a galaxy of skin cells and flour and pollen, all of it suspended, all of it burning gold for a moment before settling again on the linoleum.

You start with the pantry because it feels safest. Canned goods. A bag of rice with a twist-tie. But even here, personality crowds the shelves. A jar of pickled beets with handwriting on the label—July 2019—in a script that slants left because of the way she held her wrist after the arthritis set in. You remember that wrist. You remember being six, holding that hand across the parking lot of the A&P, how the skin felt like tissue paper over a fork. You wanted to let go because you were embarrassed by your own need, but you held on because she was walking fast and you had to keep up.

The living room is where the photographs live. Not in albums—she never had the patience for albums—but layered in shoeboxes, each print sliding against the next with a sound like a card sharp shuffling a deck. Here is your father at twenty, leaning against a car that was already old when the picture was taken. Here is a dog you never met. Here is you, eight or nine, standing in a sprinkler in shorts that are too big, your mouth open in a laugh that you can almost hear but not quite. The almost is the worst part. You hold the picture up to the light, trying to catch the frequency of that happiness, but photographs don't store sound. They store the lie of a moment that seemed important enough to arrest.

Upstairs, the beds are made. Hospital corners. She learned that in 1952, working at the motor inn on Route 9, and she never unlearned it. You sit on the edge of the mattress and the springs protest in F-sharp. The closet smells of cedar and the particular chemical vanilla of old perfume. You pull out a coat with fur trim and bury your face in it, inhaling deeply, greedily, like a diver coming up for air. It doesn't smell like her. It smells like mothballs and storage. The body is the first thing to go, even from fabric, even from air. You hang it back up, ashamed of your own hunger.

In the desk, you find the utility bills organized by year, rubber-banded with the tidy compulsiveness of someone who lived through the Depression. You find a rosary with blue glass beads, the chain tangled like a fishing line. You find a letter from a man named Frank, written in 1968, that references a weekend in Provincetown that you are fairly sure your grandfather never knew about. The handwriting is young, urgent, the i's dotted with circles. You read it twice. This is the danger of cleaning out a house: you become a trespasser in someone else's interior life, a voyeur in the corridors of choices that made them who they were. You fold the letter back into its envelope and put it in your pocket, not to keep but to decide about later, when you are not so raw, when you can consider the possibility that she was a person before she was your grandmother.

The basement is where the workbench is. Here, the smell is different—oil, sawdust, the mineral tang of old plumbing. A jar of nails sorted by size. A coffee can full of sand that served as an ashtray for decades. On the pegboard, outlines of tools that are no longer hanging there, ghosts in dust. You run your finger along the workbench and come away with a line of gray. You were not allowed down here as a child. It was his space, and then it was hers after he went, and now it is nobody's space, which means it is becoming the house's space, the way a shipwreck becomes the ocean's architecture. You flip the light switch off and on, off and on, watching the fluorescent tube struggle to stay alive.

You take a break on the back steps. The hydrangeas have gone feral, blue heads heavy and nodding toward the crabgrass. A neighbor's cat watches you from the fence with the flat suspicion of animals that know the house is in transition. You eat a sandwich you brought from the outside world, and it tastes too vivid—too alive—for this place. The bread is too soft. The mustard too sharp. You chew slowly, feeling like an invader eating rations on occupied ground. A breeze moves through the oak tree, and for a moment you hear her voice calling you in for dinner, not from any specific memory but from the air itself, the way sound haunts a valley.

By late afternoon, you have three piles: keep, donate, trash. The trash pile is largest, which feels like a betrayal but also like mercy. You cannot be the curator of every object that accumulated over eighty years. You cannot be the museum of someone else's daily rituals. The donate pile is full of coats, shoes, a blender still in its box. The keep pile is insultingly small. A ceramic bird. The rosary. A stack of recipes written on index cards, stained with oil and annotated in margins: more garlic, less salt, don't let Tom watch the pot he stirs too much.

You carry the trash bags to the curb. The sun is lowering, turning the street amber. A kid on a bicycle rides past, popping a wheelie, and for a second the present rushes in like a tide, cold and real. You are not a child sorting through a grandmother's house. You are an adult. You are the one who remains. The bags scrape against the driveway, heavy with the weight of things that were once needed and are needed no longer. Your shoulders ache. The ache feels earned.

When you lock up, the key turns smooth this time. You have disturbed the mechanism, loosened it. The house breathes differently now. You have taken things out. You have let air in. You stand on the porch and look through the window at the living room, now half-empty, the light from the streetlamp falling in a parallelogram across the floor where the rug used to be. It is still her house. It will always be her house, even when the new owners paint the kitchen yellow and tear out the cedar closet. But tonight, it is a house between stories, holding its breath, waiting to become someone else's memory.

You get in your car and do not cry. Not yet. The crying will come at a traffic light three days from now, or when you try to make her potato salad and the knife rings against the cutting board in the same key as her laugh. For now, you start the engine. You look in the rearview mirror. The house recedes, porch light burning like a small star in the gathering dark, not guiding you home but marking the place where home once was, precisely, unbearably, exactly there.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain started at three in the afternoon, which is significant only because that's when the light shifts from white to gold, and the two colors met in the wet air like old friends uncertain whether to embrace or simply nod. I was standing by the window, watching the neighbor's cat shelter beneath a hydrangea bush, its fur darkening in patches. There is something about the way animals endure weather that has always humiliated me slightly—they accept discomfort with a grace I have never mastered. I reach for umbrellas, for jackets, for explanations and excuses, while the cat simply sits and waits, attending to the moment with a patience that predates language.

I found a box of photographs last Tuesday. They were in the crawlspace, sealed in a plastic bin that smelled of attic heat and cedar. My mother’s handwriting on the lid, faded but meticulous: “1987–1993.” I sat on the unfinished plywood and lifted the lid with the care usually reserved for coffins. Inside, faces I had forgotten were waiting. Not gone, just parked in the chemical dark, waiting for light to reanimate them. A girl in a yellow raincoat—was that me? The expression was foreign. Not happy, not sad. Just fully present in a way that adulthood seems to methodically erode. The eyes were looking at the camera but also through it, at the person holding it, trusting absolutely that the world would remain standing for the 1/125th of a second required to capture her.

We don’t think about trust when we’re young. We assume the floor, the language, the love. It is only later that we realize everything is a construction, a consensus, a bridge built while we walk across it. We learn to test each step. We learn that presence is a skill that atrophies.

The cat has moved. Now it sits in the open, washing a paw. The rain has not stopped; it has simply been downgraded to an inconvenience the cat refuses to acknowledge. I admire this. I want to be the person who steps out into the rain because cleanliness or hunger or simple curiosity demands it, not the person who watches from behind glass, inventing reasons to stay dry. I have spent years inventing reasons. The right time, the right plan, the right version of myself. As if life were a recipe and not a fire.

In one of the photos, my father holds a fish. He is grinning with the particular triumph of a man who does not often grin. The fish is modest, probably not worth the effort of the photograph, but the camera has made it monumental. That is the sorcery of photography—it aggrandizes the ordinary, freezes the ephemeral, and then we look back and mistake the frame for the life. My father died in 2014. The fish died in 1989. Only the grin remains, suspended in gelatin and dye, becoming less him every year I look at it. Memory is not retrieval; it is re-creation. Each time I summon that smile, I am painting over the original with the brush of who I am now. In ten years, that grin will mean something else. In twenty, it may mean nothing at all. The photograph will persist, but the haunting will change its address.

I have been thinking about the physics of haunting. Not ghosts in sheets, but the way a house remembers footsteps. The way a stair creaks at 2 AM with the weight of someone who used to descend for water. The way a song can pull a whole room out of the air—carpet, curtains, the smell of someone’s shampoo from 2006. We are haunted by architecture, by melody, by weather. This rain was a Tuesday rain in March when I was twenty-three and walking home from a bookstore job, convinced I would be a poet. The water soaked through my canvas shoes and I felt holy, chosen by discomfort, baptized by the ordinary. Now the same frequency of drops against the pane makes me think of gutters, of insurance, of the leak I need to fix in the mudroom before the drywall rots.

Age trades resonance for maintenance. It is a fair trade, I suppose, but sometimes I miss the cathedral of my own youth, when every moment tolled with significance and every stranger was a potential witness to my becoming.

The neighbor’s cat is gone. The hydrangea trembles under the weight of water, releasing a small avalanche onto the soil below. The bush does not complain. The soil does not complain. Only humans complain, because only humans have the capacity to imagine a dryer reality and then grieve the distance between here and there. We are cursed and blessed with the subjunctive mood, the realm of might-have-been, which is the most populated country on earth.

I put the photographs back, but not before slipping one into my pocket—the girl in the yellow raincoat. I want to carry her like a talisman. I want to ask her what she was thinking, what she believed came next. She would not answer, of course. She is busy being. She is busy standing in a puddle, feeling the cold seep through her boots, utterly unconcerned with the narrative that will later be woven around her. She does not know she is a memory. She does not know she is a ghost. She does not know that one day she will stand at a window, looking out at a different rain, trying to remember what it felt like to be so thoroughly inside her own life.

The rain is slowing. The gold has overtaken the white, and the wet world gleams with that false renewal that follows a storm. Soon the sun will drop, and the gold will turn to copper, then to grey, then to the blue-black of a sky that has forgotten the day. I will make dinner. I will listen to the news. I will perform the small rituals that keep the house from becoming a museum, keep the present from surrendering entirely to the past.

But for now, I stand at the window with a dead girl in my pocket and a live cat somewhere in the wet green, and I feel the strange, suspended grief of being alive in a world that continues to be beautiful despite everything we have done to it, and to each other. The rain knows nothing of loss. It falls. It gathers. It runs to the sea and rises again. It is the only immortality we can touch, cycling through sky and earth, indifferent and intimate.

I want to step outside. I want to let my hair darken in patches. I want to sit beneath the hydrangea and wait, without knowing what I am waiting for, trusting the world to remain standing for just a little longer, trusting the girl I was to forgive the woman I have become, trusting the rain to wash us both clean.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain began without announcement, the kind that doesn't thunder but simply arrives, as if the sky had been holding its breath and finally let go. I was sitting by the window when it started, watching the light shift from gold to grey in the span of a breath. There is a particular silence that comes just before the rain reaches the ground—a hush, a waiting—and then the sound fills everything, not loud but omnipresent, like blood in the ears. It is a frequency that tunes out the higher pitches of anxiety, a natural white noise that resets something essential in the brain.

My coffee had gone cold. I had not noticed. The cup sat on the windowsill leaving a pale ring on the wood, and I thought about how everything leaves a mark, however faint. The wood will remember the moisture long after I wipe it away. The house will remember my weight on the floorboards, the particular creak of my step in the hallway at two in the morning when I cannot sleep. We are all making impressions on the world simply by existing, eroding it softly, being eroded in return. Even now, my fingerprints grease the glass; even now, my breath fogs a small circle that evaporates slowly, leaving nothing visible but taking something with it nonetheless.

I remember being seven years old and standing barefoot on our porch during a storm like this one. My mother had called me inside but I lingered at the threshold, where the overhang kept the dry concrete sacred while the yard beyond turned to dark soup. The boundary fascinated me—the exact line where safety became wildness. I put one toe across and felt the shocking cold of the water, then pulled back, then crossed again, playing at the edge of something I couldn't name. It was not defiance. It was curiosity about the moment of transition, the exact instant when dry became wet, when inside became out.

We spend so much of our lives in those thresholds without recognizing them. The moment before a name is spoken and a relationship changes forever. The second before the doctor speaks. The breath between sleeping and waking when you don't yet know who you are, where you are, whether the sorrow or joy you carry is real or dreamed. We think of life as a series of rooms with solid walls, but really it is all doorways, all liminal spaces where the temperature hasn't decided what it wants to be.

The rain is heavier now. It sheets down the glass, warping the view of the oak tree in the yard. The leaves have turned the silver-green they only become in downpours, underside-up, trembling. The tree is a hundred years old, maybe more. It has stood through wars I read about in books, through winters that cracked pavement, through the construction of this house and the one before it. It does not remember these events because a tree does not remember the way we do, yet it carries them in its rings, in the lean of its trunk, in the particular architecture of its branches where lightning struck once and the wood knuckled and grew around the wound. Memory without consciousness. History without grief. I have a book open on the table behind me, pages riffling in the draft, and I think of how paper was once pulp, once a tree like this one, breathing. Everything transforms; nothing is lost, only translated.

I wonder sometimes what the tree makes of me, if it makes anything at all. Perhaps I am simply a moving shadow behind glass, a temporary warmth, a flickering at night when I pace the kitchen. We are so brief, compared to oaks. And yet within that brevity we contain multitudes—every version of ourselves that ever was, layered like sediment. The child on the porch. The adolescent who believed pain was permanent. The adult learning that most things pass, even the ones we wish wouldn't. The self I was this morning, already obsolete, already becoming a story I will tell differently next year.

The neighbor's cat has taken shelter under my porch. I can see her through the gap in the boards, a grey comma curled around herself. She does not worry about the rain ending. She does not think about what she will do when it stops, does not make contingency plans or dwell on the inadequacy of her shelter. There is only the dry space, the sound above, the warmth of her own body, the steady rhythm of her breathing. I envy this. Not because I think ignorance is preferable, but because there is a kind of intelligence in acceptance that humans have largely abandoned in favor of constant prognostication. We are always elsewhere, preparing, regretting, anticipating. The cat is here. The cat is only here.

My phone buzzes on the table, a wasp against glass. I ignore it. The world will continue its spinning without my immediate attention; this is a lesson I am slowly, stubbornly learning. There is a tyranny to being constantly available, a subtle violence done to the present moment. The rain asks nothing of me. It does not need my opinion or my efficiency or my carefully worded opinions. It simply falls, and in doing so, grants me permission to simply be, to occupy space without justifying the occupation.

I think about all the people I have been. They do not disappear; they accumulate. I carry the child who was afraid of the dark, the young man who thought love was rescue, the person I was yesterday who believed a different set of worries would define the rest of his life. We are geological, really. Each year lays down a stratum. Sometimes there are earthquakes—loss, joy, trauma, grace—that shift the layers, fold them, bring what was buried to the surface dark and rich. We are never only who we are right now. We are the entire excavation, the whole canyon, the bedrock and the topsoil and the artifacts buried in between.

The coffee ring has dried on the windowsill, a pale ochre halo. I should clean it, but I don't. Let it stay as proof that I was here, that time passed, that the afternoon existed and was not entirely wasted on productivity. We are desperate for evidence. Photographs, scars, stains on wood, the worn spot on a book's spine. We know how quickly everything erodes, how the tide comes in regardless of what we've built, how even mountains become sand. The ring is a small resistance against that forgetting.

The rain will stop. The cat will leave, shaking each paw with precise disgust. I will pick up my phone and answer the buzzing and re-enter the stream of obligation and noise and the bright, flat light of screens. But for now, there is this: the grey light, the water on glass, the breathing of the house around me, the pages turning themselves. For now, I am sitting in the doorway between what was and what will be, one toe in the cold, one foot on the dry porch, fully alive in the threshold, waiting with the world.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The drawer in my childhood bedroom no longer opens smoothly. The wood has swollen with decades of coastal humidity, and the brass handle wobbles if you pull too hard. Inside, beneath a stack of outdated swim goggles and a broken music box, there is a shoebox of letters I never sent. Some are addressed to people who have died. Others to people I never met. A few are simply addressed to the house itself, as if the walls might absorb the words and redistribute them through the floorboards, into the garden, up through the oak tree that still shades the kitchen window.

I found the box last Tuesday while helping my mother move a dresser. She didn't ask what was inside. She never asks. She has her own boxes, her own unopened envelopes of time. We carried the furniture in silence, the kind of silence that exists only between people who have shared the same roof for too long and not long enough.

The first letter I opened was dated July 14, 2003. I was twelve. The handwriting is unmistakably mine—aggressive loops, the *y*'s descending like anchors into the next line. It was written to my future self. I had demanded to know whether we had become an astronaut. I had outlined the specific requirements: must have visited the moon, must own a dog named Galileo, must never eat peas again. There was a diagram of a rocket ship in the margin. The paper smelled like the lemon tree that used to grow outside my window before the blight took it.

I did not become an astronaut. I do not own a dog. I still eat peas, though I prefer them now with butter and mint.

The disappointment I expected to feel never arrived. Instead, there was a tenderness, as if the twelve-year-old me had not issued a mandate but had simply been checking in, making sure I was still here, still breathing, still capable of wonder. The rocket ship was drawn with such care—the windows perfectly circular, the flames erupting from the base in violent orange crayon. I had believed so completely in the possibility of everything.

The second letter was to a girl named Sarah who moved away in eighth grade. We had been best friends for exactly four months, which at that age is a lifetime. She left in October, right when the monarch butterflies were passing through on their way to Mexico. She said she would write. I wrote her seventeen letters. I described the monarchs clustering in the eucalyptus grove behind the school, how the branches looked like they were bearing leaves of flame. I told her about my parents' fights, coding the details in symbols we had invented—thunder meant shouting, rain meant silence, hail meant something broken. I never mailed them because I didn't have her address in Boise, or Portland, or wherever the moving truck had taken her. It seemed impossible that the postal service could bridge such a distance.

Reading them now, I am struck by how much I needed someone to see me. The letters are desperate with observation. I documented everything: the way Mr. Henderson's toupee shifted in the wind during fire drills, the exact number of steps from the school gate to the ice cream shop (347), the texture of the cafeteria's mashed potatoes (library paste, slightly warm). I wanted Sarah to have the map of my world, as if by giving her the coordinates, she could teleport back.

I wonder where she is now. Probably not thinking of monarchs or library paste. Maybe she has her own shoebox, her own unsent cartography.

There is a letter to my father, written the night after I graduated high school. It is angry. It is three pages of accusation wrapped in the careful language of someone who has read too many Victorian novels. I accused him of being absent, of loving his boat more than his family, of speaking to my mother in a tone I could only describe as "administrative." I said I would never forgive him for missing my graduation ceremony. He had been in the hospital that day, getting his gallbladder removed, but at eighteen I had decided that physical ailments were merely convenient excuses for emotional failures.

I never gave it to him. He died two years later, not from anything related to his gallbladder, but from a stroke that arrived like a thief in the early morning, stealing him from his coffee and his newspaper. The letter stayed in the box. I wonder if he would have recognized himself in it. Probably not. We never see ourselves in other people's indictments. We see sketches, caricatures, versions of us that have been filtered through someone else's need.

The last letter I read before my mother called me downstairs for lunch was written on a train from Paris to Lyon in 2019. It was addressed to no one. It was a description of the woman sitting across from me in the dining car. She was in her seventies, wearing a lavender coat that had seen better decades. She was peeling an orange with a small silver knife, and the smell filled the compartment like a childhood memory. She caught me watching and smiled. She said, in English accented with something Mediterranean, "You should write this down. The way the light hits the vineyards. No one believes me when I tell them how green they are."

I wrote it down. I wrote about her hands, mapped with veins like rivers on antique paper. I wrote about the orange, how she divided it into halves, then quarters, then eighths, as if performing surgery. I wrote about the way she ate each segment with her eyes closed, savoring not just the taste but the fact of it, the miracle of sweetness in a world that so often offers bitterness.

I wrote that the green of the vineyards made me want to believe in God, not the God of churches, but the God of small, insistent beauties. The God of shoeboxes and swollen drawers. The God of unsent letters that wait like seeds, patient and unlikely, for the right season to grow.

My mother made tomato soup and grilled cheese, the same lunch she has made since I was six. We ate at the kitchen table, looking out at the oak tree. I didn't tell her about the box. Some things need to stay in the dark a little longer, composting, becoming soil for whatever comes next.

But I will go back. I will read the rest. And perhaps I will finally send a few—not to the dead, not to the lost, but to the living. To my mother, maybe, though she is sitting right here. To the woman on the train, if I could find her. To my twelve-year-old self, assuring him that the moon is still there, still waiting, and that peas, when prepared with enough butter, are not so bad after all.

There are 347 steps from the school gate to the ice cream shop. I checked, last time I was home. The ice cream shop is gone now, replaced by a pharmacy. But the steps remain. The distance remains. We measure our lives in such increments—in letters sent and unsent, in the space between what we meant to say and what we actually did.

The drawer sticks. The handle wobbles. But it still opens. It still opens.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The last time I was here, the oak tree was still bleeding sap where the swing had been. Now the rope is gone and the bark has sealed into a rough, dark eye. It watches me as I climb the porch steps, each one groaning a slightly different pitch, like a broken xylophone.

Inside, the house smells of cinnamon and plaster dust. My mother left the windows cracked, and a family of sparrows has colonized the upstairs bathroom, weaving sticks into the shower rod. I should shoo them out. Instead, I stand in the hallway and listen to their small, frantic negotiations. They sound like the house is keeping a secret.

I’ve come to sort. That’s the word we use—*sort*—as if memories can be organized into bins: keep, donate, trash. The trash bin is already overflowing in the driveway, black plastic yawning at the sky. I haven’t put anything in it yet.

In the kitchen, the drawer sticks, then gives. Inside: rubber bands fossilized into figure-eights, a collection of takeout menus for restaurants that closed during the Bush administration, and a photograph of my grandfather holding a fish the size of a forearm. He is grinning with his whole face, the way people do when they are surprised by their own luck. I don’t remember him ever grinning like that in real life. Real life was quieter, a series of grunts and newspaper rustles. Maybe the fish taught him something temporary. Maybe that’s what we’re all hunting—moments that teach us how to grin without thinking.

I had planned to be methodical. Room by room. Systematic. But method implies a known destination, and I am navigating by feel, by scent, by the particular frequency of a floorboard’s creak under my left foot. The house is a map of itself, and I am lost in the best way.

The basement stairs descend into a throat of darkness. I pull the string for the bulb, and the shadows retreat to the corners, where they huddle among mason jars and the rusted remains of a stationary bike. There is a trunk here I have never seen opened. The latch is orange with rust but yields to pressure. Inside: quilts. Not the good quilts—these are the practice quilts, the ones made from flour sacks and failed dye lots, stitched by a great-aunt who apparently believed that purple and mustard yellow were natural companions. I lift one to my face. It smells of cedar and something sweeter, like overripe apples.

I spread it over the back of the sofa. The stitches are uneven, some pulling tight, others looping loose as laughter. I think of her hands, knuckled with arthritis, moving with a stubborn precision. She made this not because she needed another quilt, but because the act of cutting and pinning and pulling thread through cloth was a way of speaking when language felt too dangerous, too definitive. I fold it against my chest and decide immediately that the categories of *keep* and *donate* are insufficient. There should be a category called *carry*.

I carry the quilt upstairs.

In the living room, the piano is still in tune, which seems like a miracle or a lie. I press middle C. The sound is clear and sad, a single raindrop in a dry season. I don’t play. I only wanted to confirm that sound could still live here, that the house hadn’t forgotten how to resonate.

On top of the piano: a glass bowl full of sea glass. Green, white, a single rare blue. My grandmother spent twenty years walking the same quarter-mile of beach in Maine, pocketing the ocean’s debris. She called it treasure. I thought it was litter until I was twelve and she showed me how to hold a piece to the sun so the light turned translucent, revealing the swirls and bubbles trapped inside. “That’s the moment it cooled,” she said. “Right there. You’re looking at a specific second from 1920, or 1955, or whenever this bottle was made and broken.” I remember being furious that I couldn’t see the exact year. I wanted data. Now I understand that specificity is a myth we tell ourselves to feel in control. The bubble doesn’t know when it was born. It only knows it is caught.

I sit on the piano bench and let the sea glass sift through my fingers. The blue piece is the size of a nickel.
